one_line_takeaway: Even fully benign prompts endanger LLM-generated code: as functional-requirement quality drops from expert-level to novice-level, vulnerability rates climb sharply (e.g. CWE-703 23%->59%) across 10 models and three languages, and Chain-of-Thought / self-correction prompting measurably claws much of that risk back.
key_finding: As prompt normativity decreases from L0 to L3, vulnerability rate increases consistently across all 10 models and 8 CWE pillars, and the effect is strongest for logic/control-flow-intensive weaknesses. CWE-703 (improper handling of exceptional conditions) rises from 23.12% at L0 to 58.59% at L3 (>150% increase) and CWE-284 (improper access control) from 13.59% to 49.84%. Recall/pattern-driven categories are more resilient: CWE-693 rises only modestly, 31.87% -> 38.00%. CWE-707 is non-monotonic (lower at L2, 27.12%, than at L0/L1) because clear L0/L1 injection prompts steer models toward the insecure 'shortcut' (e.g. string concatenation). The trend survives both perturbation tracks, both extra languages, majority-vote re-scoring, and temperature changes. Mitigation works: CoT and RE-ACT self-correction cut vulnerability rates, most on the hardest cases -- e.g. CWE-284 L3 49.84% -> 43.41% (CoT) / 42.41% (RE-ACT); CWE-693 L0 31.87% -> 24.88% (CoT) / 18.58% (RE-ACT); CWE-707 L0 31.25% -> 18.61% (CoT) / 14.59% (RE-ACT). Larger/more capable models tend to show a stronger normativity effect (higher vulnerability under weak prompts), interpreted as a conditional association, not a causal size effect.
core_contribution: The paper isolates a specific, non-adversarial cause of insecure LLM-generated code: the normativity (quality as a functional-requirements specification) of an otherwise benign user prompt. Its own contributions are (1) a quantifiable three-dimensional model of prompt normativity along Goal Clarity, Information Completeness, and Logical Consistency, operationalized as four levels L0 (fully normative, 'expert engineer') to L3 (highly non-normative, 'programming novice'); (2) CWE-BENCH-PYTHON, a hand-authored benchmark of 8 Pillar-level CWEs -> 33 Base-level CWEs -> 165 single-function Python tasks, each with an L0-L3 prompt set (660 primary prompts) plus two semantic-preserving perturbations each (1,980 evaluation instances), extended to Java and C++; (3) large-scale empirical evidence, across 10 mainstream LLMs, that vulnerability rate rises monotonically as prompt normativity falls; and (4) a demonstration that Chain-of-Thought and a SELF-REFINE-style 'Regenerate Act' (RE-ACT) self-correction strategy substantially reduce the induced vulnerabilities. By design, every prompt at every level EXCLUDES explicit or implicit security directives and unsafe implementation cues, so all security reasoning is left to the model, isolating prompt normativity as the sole independent variable.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model. The paper explicitly excludes malicious prompts, prompt-injection, and deliberately omitted security directives (contrasting itself with that prior line of work). The 'risk' studied is accidental/unintentional: a well-meaning developer writes a vague, incomplete, or logically inconsistent functional requirement, and the LLM, forced to 'fill in gaps' and 'resolve contradictions', silently makes unsafe assumptions (e.g., choosing the 'simplest' path such as string concatenation for an injection-prone task) and emits exploitable code. The target vulnerabilities are standard CWE weakness classes (SQL injection, XSS/CWE-707 improper neutralization, CWE-284 improper access control, CWE-703 improper handling of exceptional conditions, CWE-693 protection-mechanism failure, etc.).
limitations: (1) Inter-rater agreement is explicitly a placeholder: the paper's own note states the Fleiss' kappa values in Table 1 (overall 0.89) are placeholders, to be recomputed in the final version using three experts plus GPT-5.2 -- so the reported reliability figure is not final. (2) Vulnerability labels come from an LLM-based detector (GPT-4o / majority-vote of LLM judges), not from ground-truth exploits or human audit of every instance, so both false positives and false negatives in the security scoring are possible. (3) Single-function, single-shot granularity omits inter-procedural, multi-turn, RAG, agentic, and repository-level contexts that dominate real development, and the paper concedes some C/C++ vulnerabilities cannot be judged without call-site context, producing non-monotonic exceptions. (4) Cross-family scale-vs-vulnerability trend confounds parameter count with architecture, training data, and alignment. (5) The self-correction mitigation relies only on the model's own internal knowledge, so its ceiling is bounded by the model's security knowledge. (6) Proprietary model versions in Table 2 lack exact parameter counts, and one workflow figure lists a partly different model set (e.g., Llama3.3-70B, DeepSeek Coder-33B, Claude 3.7) than the Table 2 pool of 10, a minor inconsistency.

datasets_used: CWE-BENCH-PYTHON (authors' own): 8 Pillar CWEs, 33 Base CWEs, 165 single-function Python tasks, 4 normativity levels = 660 primary prompts; +2 perturbation variants each = 1,980 evaluation instances
Java and C/C++ extensions of the same tasks (same single-function paradigm, replicated only where equivalent functionality is achievable)
8 Pillar CWEs covered: CWE-284, CWE-664, CWE-682, CWE-691, CWE-693, CWE-697, CWE-703, CWE-707
quantitative_results: Prompt-normativity compliance audit: 636/660 prompts pass = 96.36% overall; Fleiss' kappa 0.89 (explicitly a placeholder value per the paper's own note)
CWE-703 vulnerability rate: 23.12% (L0) -> 58.59% (L3), an increase of over 150%
CWE-682 (low-difficulty) near-flat and low: ~4.4-4.8% across L0-L3
Detector threshold: an instance is labeled vulnerable only at confidence > 0.9; vulnerability rate computed only over functionally correct code
baselines_compared: 10 Models Under Test: open-source CodeQwen1.5-7B, Mixtral-8x7B (MoE), Qwen3-14B, StarCoder2-15B, Qwen3-32B, Kimi-K2 (MoE); proprietary GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Grok-3, Claude-Sonnet-4
Prompting/generation methods compared: Basic (single-shot) vs Chain-of-Thought (CoT) vs Regenerate Act / RE-ACT self-correction (SELF-REFINE-inspired)

Internal comparison axes: normativity levels L0 vs L1 vs L2 vs L3; languages Python vs Java vs C/C++; decoding temperature T=0 / 0.4 / 0.8; judge protocol single-detector (GPT-4o) vs 3-judge majority vote (DeepSeek-V3.1, GPT-4o, Grok-3). No external competing benchmark/system is used as a performance baseline.
